## [00:00 - 03:01]

Hi, this is Don Crowther with a video about scripting your webinar. So the question that every person who does webinar has to answer is what's the best way for you? And I underline you here because it's different for every person to know what you want to say at each point in your webinar. So there are a number of different ways of going about this. You can wing it if you want to, but my recommendation is that you create some kind of base script format, some kind of way of letting yourself know what you want to say, where you want to say it in a webinar. The reason we do this is because oftentimes when you're winging it at the end of it, you sit back and you say, oh, if only I would have done this and this and this and that comment was in the wrong place. Let's take the advantage of time and clear thinking and combine that with the advantage of the adrenaline that flows when we're doing a presentation. And so that we get a carefully thought out process of taking someone through the psychology necessary to prepare them to buy. So my question for you is what's your best base script format? What's your best way of letting yourself know what you want to say at any given point in your webinar? Let me show you several options. The first one is to have a slide deck like you're seeing right here. So it's a this one happens to be PowerPoint. It could be keynote. It can be any kind of different, different presentation tool where you've got a set of points that are being made on the screen. And you can use that as the outline to tell yourself what it is that you want to be saying. The next is that same slide deck, but add notes to it. I'll show you how we do that, what those look like in a moment that allow you to remember without having to remember what it is that you wanted to say at that particular point. The next is a slide deck same thing with notes, but this time we're adding some sections of scripted content, literally word for word, or at least some phrases that remind you of the key actual words that you want to use at any point during the presentation. This is a way of capitalizing on the fluidity of presenting, along with making sure you get the right words at exactly the right time, because you might only script 10% of your presentation, or three slides, or something like that. But that way you make sure that those particular

## [03:01 - 06:03]

sections are said exactly the way you want them to be said. Another one is a word for word script from which you riff. So I actually have a couple of friends who literally write every single word of their webinar out. Personally, that's not me. I find it takes way too much time, but it is, does have the advantage that you get all the elements of the psychology, all everything you want to say exactly worked out in advance. And then it just comes out in your presentation. My recommendation is to choose the one that fits your own personal style. Personally, I use the slide deck with scripted sections and notes method. So I will oftentimes go through and put in so the bulk of my presentation is just slides. But then I'll have a few slides where I'll have some notes, and I'll have another few slides where I actually script out word for word, because I've learned that I work really well from an outline, but sometimes I need to give myself notes. And sometimes I actually perform better if I give myself a word for word script to ensure that I use exactly the right words at certain points in the webinar, I tend to script certain parts of my presentation like the introduction. Why do I do that? Because oftentimes left my own devices, I end up telling three stories during introduction, all of a sudden I've used 20 minutes my presentation, and nobody's gotten anything and people are leaving because they're saying this is a content free presentation. And so I oftentimes will script my introduction so as it's gives the bare minimum of what they need to be prepared to buy eventually. I script the pivot because it is such an essential part of the entire webinar. And every phrase in the webinar determines whether people continue to watch during the cells offer part of the webinar. I struck I tend to script the second level benefits. And this is because I sometimes get too wordy and flowery in that section. So it starts sounding like an infomercial, not only because I say a lot, but I say it in a way that sounds like this is the greatest things in sight slice bread. And I don't want to sound that way. That's not realistic. And so I script it so that I carefully manage exactly what's going to come out of my mouth during that section. Another place where I have the same kind of thing is the features of my product section. I do this because I have found that if I don't, I'm so excited about the product I've created, I want to tell people about it. And oftentimes I'll go on for a long period of time. And don't add any value during that time. So I script so I don't describe those too much. I script the show your URL section because that's the the the pinnacle point of the

## [06:03 - 09:06]

entire webinar. And at that point, I want to carefully control exactly what I'm going to say. I'll script how I'll respond to key objections. So there are certain things that I just know people are going to ask. And so I actually script out my answers to those key questions. Now, not so much questions as objections, because oftentimes the the objection to buying is couched as a question. And so I just have those in a script, maybe on a three by five card sitting in front of me, maybe in a note section on a PowerPoint, wherever I've got a set of answers that I pre prepare and think about carefully to be able to answer those tough things so that I don't find myself saying those one or two phrases that can kill your cells. And I script the emotional close. And the reason for that is again, I tend to go long, I tend to emphasize too much, or get too deep if I'm not careful. So ultimately, here's the way I look at it. ## [09:06 - 10:13]

so that they sound the same. Now that takes practice. Now as you do more webinars, you're going to find your needs changing. Some parts you're going to need less notes. So I've done thousands of presentations like the one that you're seeing on your screen right now. And so now I write far fewer notes than I ever did before. And some points, you're going to absolutely want to script so you can get them perfectly. In my case, I oftentimes do it because I tend to use too many words. I get off on tangents. In other cases, it may be that you blank out and don't remember what it is you wanted to say. In other places, it may be you definitely want to get word for word right because you're in somewhat tenuous or a key part where you're dealing with something that may be an objection that could actually hurt your cells. And so you want to script that out. But whatever you do, as time goes on, you'll get better. And as the time goes on, you'll figure out exactly what it is that works best for you.
